Deep-fried Puffed Bread with Spiced Potato

english: Fried Indian Bread with Potato Curry
Deep-fried Puffed Bread with Spiced Potato

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term for poori bhaji combination; closest_parent is fried bread food product (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for poori bhaji; A0CKP (Unleavened bread) is the closest defensible parent for the poori component. Bhaji (spiced potato curry) component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for poori bhaji as a combined dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of poori bhaji consists of 2 pieces of deep-fried puffed whole wheat bread (approximately 40–50 g each) accompanied by a portion of spiced potato curry (bhaji), totalling approximately 200 g.

nutrient_source.title: Poori (Puri) and Aloo Bhaji — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for poori (deep-fried whole wheat bread) and aloo bhaji (spiced potato curry) components. A weighted composite was calculated: approximately 45% poori and 55% bhaji by weight, consistent with a 2-piece serving. ICMR-NIN reports poori at approximately 336 kcal/100 g and aloo bhaji at approximately 97 kcal/100 g; the composite yields approximately 200 kcal/100 g. All secondary nutrients are similarly composited from ICMR-NIN component data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat-based deep-fried bread (puri/poori) — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entry for chapati/roti and fried wheat bread products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for poori bhaji as a combined dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for deep-fried wheat bread products and chapati (GI range 52–70 for wheat-based Indian breads). The potato bhaji component (boiled potato GI ~78, but spiced and mixed preparation lowers effective GI) moderates the composite. A composite GI of approximately 62 (medium) is defensible for this dish. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 55 g carbohydrate in 200 g serving) / 100 = 34.1, rounded to 34.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and deep-frying oil load
hypertension.guidance: Poori bhaji contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g and is deep-fried, contributing to a moderate sodium and saturated-fat load; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and request reduced salt in the bhaji preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index and high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 62 and a glycemic load of approximately 34 per standard 2-piece serving, poori bhaji causes a significant postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict to one piece, pair with a high-fibre side, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying fat content and total energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Poori is deep-fried in vegetable oil, contributing approximately 8.5 g fat per 100 g including 1.2 g saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency and prefer baked or air-fried alternatives where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Poori Bhaji is a classic North Indian breakfast and brunch dish consisting of two deep-fried puffed whole wheat breads (poori) served alongside a spiced potato curry (bhaji). The poori is made from whole wheat flour (atta) kneaded into a stiff dough, rolled into small discs, and deep-fried in hot oil until it puffs up into a golden, crisp-yet-soft sphere. The bhaji is a dry or semi-dry curry made from boiled potatoes tempered with mustard seeds, cumin, turmeric, green chilli, onion, and fresh coriander. Together, the dish provides a satisfying combination of textures — the light, airy crunch of the poori against the soft, spiced potato filling. Nutritionally, a 2-piece serving (approximately 200 g) delivers around 400 kcal, 9 g protein, 17 g fat, and 55 g carbohydrate, making it an energy-dense meal. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal. It is widely consumed across India, particularly in the states of Punjab, Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Gujarat, and is a staple offering at Indian vegetarian restaurants in Singapore such as Jewel of India. Clinically, the medium glycemic index (~62) and high glycemic load (~34 per serving) make it a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in moderation. The deep-frying process increases total fat and caloric density, which is relevant for cardiovascular disease and weight management. The dish provides useful amounts of dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g), iron (1.8 mg/100 g), and B vitamins from the whole wheat component.
common_user_queries: How many calories in poori bhaji?
Is poori bhaji suitable for diabetics?
What is puri bhaji made of?
What is bhaji in Indian food?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are composited from ICMR-NIN data for poori and aloo bhaji components using a defensible weight ratio. No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for the combined dish as served at this restaurant. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat-bread entries. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite of ICMR-NIN poori and aloo bhaji entries (approximately 45% poori, 55% bhaji by weight). Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for 2 pieces poori plus bhaji portion as typically served. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN data for vegetable-oil-fried products in this category. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a vegan dish; no animal-source B12 present. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no vitamin D source in ingredients per ICMR-NIN. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for these food components. 7. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for deep-fried wheat bread and chapati products; no direct GI entry exists for poori bhaji as a combined dish. 8. Serving size (200 g) and restaurant context (Jewel of India, Boat Quay, Singapore) preserved from GrabFood source record GD002060. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN fat composition data for vegetable oil used in deep-frying and potato bhaji preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite nutrient profile from ICMR-NIN poori and aloo bhaji component data with documented weight ratio.
Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous wheat-bread entries with full documentation.
Added all mandatory multilingual names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Populated comprehensive aliases_by_language and verified consistency with flat aliases list.
Documented all zero values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) with confirmed zero justification.
Documented selenium_mcg = -1 with explicit note that it is not reported in ICMR-NIN source.
Used only approved sources: ICMR-NIN (primary regional) and University of Sydney GI Database (GI-specific).
Applied correct ontology match_type values: closest_parent for FoodOn and FoodEx2, none for SNOMED CT.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions including Type 2 Diabetes.
